coredata.dc:title: A novel nitrogen-doped carbon dots as “on-off-on” fluorescent sensor for ultrasensitive and visual quantitative detection of mercuric (II) and glutathione
coredata.dc:creator: Han, Yuxin
Bian, Yuying
Wang, Guang
coredata.dc:description: A novel nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) was prepared composed of anhydrous citric acid and 2-amino-1-H-benzimidazole with solid state method. The XPS and FT-IR spectra indicate that lot of chelating groups such as -OH, -COOH and -NH2 are attached on the surface of NCDs. NCDs exhibited the characteristic fluorescence property and its fluorescence spectra gradually red-shifted with the increase of excitation wavelengths. The prepared NCDs in water solution has highly selective “turn-off” fluorescence response towards Hg2+ over the other metal ions and common anions with a low detection limit of 3.50 μM. Furthermore, NCDs presented the successive identification and detection capability, NCDs@Hg2+ displayed highly selective “turn-on” fluorescence response towards the bioactive glutathione (GSH) over the other amino acids with a low detection limit of 3.43 μM. The “turn-off” fluorescence of NCDs was caused by the selective coordination with Hg2+ through both static and dynamic quenching mechanisms, and the fluorescence recovery can be attributed to the removal of Hg2+ from the complex NCDs@Hg2+ by GSH. The hydrogel films containing NCDs and NCDs@Hg2+ can semi-quantitatively detect Hg2+ and GSH in micromolar level with naked eyes under UV lamp, respectively. In addition, the prepared NCDs have satisfactory recovery in detection of Hg2+ and GSH in practical water samples and human serum samples. This work proves the N-doped carbon NCDs are efficient material as fluorescence chemosensors to identify and detect metal ions and bioactive substances in practical samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Fluorescence sensor
Successive detection
Hg (II)
Glutathione

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of real water samples and human serum samples
Preparation of the novel NCDs hydrogel film sensor
The “on-off” fluorescence sensing of NCDs for Hg2+ in water
The “turn-on” fluorescence sensing of NCDs@Hg2+ for GSH
Visual quantitative detection of Hg2+and GSH

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (no. 22075040 ). The authors also acknowledge the supports from Jilin Provincial Department of Education .
This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (no. 22075040). The authors also acknowledge the supports from Jilin Provincial Department of Education.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM and HRTEM images of the prepared NCDs.

(a) The XRD patterns of NCDs; (b) The FT-IR spectrum of NCDs; (c) The XPS full spectra of NCDs. (d) Photoluminescent emission spectra of NCDs in pure water (excitation wavelength from 335 nm to 425 nm).

(a) Fluorescence intensity at 425 nm of NCDs in water with different metal ions. (b) Fluorescence intensity at 425 nm of NCDs in water with different metal ions. (c) The fluorescence spectra of NCDs in water solution with the addition of different volumes of Hg2+ solution. Inset is the fluorescence color of NCDs in water with and without Hg2+. (d) The linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and Hg2+ concentration ranges from 12 to 28 μM. (λex=375 nm).

The fluorescence intensity at 425 nm of “NCDs@Hg2+” in water solution (a) with different amino acids and (b) with mixed amino acids. Inset in Fig. 4a are the fluorescence photographs of NCDs@Hg2+ with different amino acid. (c) The fluorescence spectra of “NCDs@Hg2+” in water solution with additions of different volumes of Hg2+. (d) The linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and GSH concentration ranges from 40 μM to 120 μM. (λex=375 nm).
(a) Stern-Volmer plots of the fluorescence intensities as a function of Hg2+ linear relationship curve (12–28 μmol/L) (b) Fluorescence decay curves of NCDs, NCDs+Hg2+ and NCDs@Hg2++GSH.

(a) Images of the NCDs hydrogel films in the presence of various concentrations of Hg2+ under 365 nm UV light. (b) Images of the “NCDs@Hg2+” hydrogel films (5 mL NCDs aqueous solution with 50 μL,20 mmol/L Hg2+ aqueous solution) in the presence of various concentrations of GSH under 365 nm UV light.
The synthetic route of NCDs and the mechanism for Hg2+ and GSH detection.
Detection of the spiked Hg2+ in Jing Lake water and tap water samples.
Detection of the spiked GSH in Jing Lake water and tap water samples.
Detection of the spiked Hg2+ in human serum samples.
Detection of the spiked GSH in human serum samples.

head.ce:title: A novel nitrogen-doped carbon dots as “on-off-on” fluorescent sensor for ultrasensitive and visual quantitative detection of mercuric (II) and glutathione

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Nanobiosensing and Nanobioanalysis at Universities of Jilin Province, Faculty of Chemistry, Northeast Normal University, Changchun 130024, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Nanobiosensing and Nanobioanalysis at Universities of Jilin Province, Faculty of Chemistry, Northeast Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Nanobiosensing and Nanobioanalysis at Universities of Jilin Province, Faculty of Chemistry, Northeast Normal University, Changchun, 130024, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) was prepared composed of anhydrous citric acid and 2-amino-1-H-benzimidazole with solid state method. The XPS and FT-IR spectra indicate that lot of chelating groups such as -OH, -COOH and -NH2 are attached on the surface of NCDs. NCDs exhibited the characteristic fluorescence property and its fluorescence spectra gradually red-shifted with the increase of excitation wavelengths. The prepared NCDs in water solution has highly selective “turn-off” fluorescence response towards Hg2+ over the other metal ions and common anions with a low detection limit of 3.50 μM. Furthermore, NCDs presented the successive identification and detection capability, NCDs@Hg2+ displayed highly selective “turn-on” fluorescence response towards the bioactive glutathione (GSH) over the other amino acids with a low detection limit of 3.43 μM. The “turn-off” fluorescence of NCDs was caused by the selective coordination with Hg2+ through both static and dynamic quenching mechanisms, and the fluorescence recovery can be attributed to the removal of Hg2+ from the complex NCDs@Hg2+ by GSH. The hydrogel films containing NCDs and NCDs@Hg2+ can semi-quantitatively detect Hg2+ and GSH in micromolar level with naked eyes under UV lamp, respectively. In addition, the prepared NCDs have satisfactory recovery in detection of Hg2+ and GSH in practical water samples and human serum samples. This work proves the N-doped carbon NCDs are efficient material as fluorescence chemosensors to identify and detect metal ions and bioactive substances in practical samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The excessive discharge of heavy metal ions caused by industrial expansion has seriously affected the human health and environment security [1]. Therefore, the sensing and detection of heavy metal ions are of great significance in many fields such as clinical diagnosis, biochemical medicine and environmental science. Hg (II) is one of the most prevalent toxic metal ions in environment and health concern, which can enrich in the form of food chain and absorbed by human, because it can easily pass through the biological membranes (i.e., skin, respiratory and gastrointestinal tissue), and even destroy the human body [2]. For the sake of our environment and our health, mercuric ions are necessary to be detected. So far, many methods of detecting Hg2+ ions have been developed, mainly including inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), atomic absorption spectrum (AAS), electrochemical methods and so on [3]. However, these methods are expensive, time consumption and complex operation. Hence, the rapid and simple determination of Hg2+ ions has attracted much attention.

In addition, development of highly sensitive probes for target organic molecules has been a significant research area because of their important roles in biological, industrial and environmental processes [4]. For example, glutathione (GSH), a thiol containing tripeptide, plays a vital role in biological systems for maintaining the appropriate oxidation–reduction state. GSH can help maintain normal immune system functions and has antioxidant effect [5]. Moreover, GSH is an important component in our diet and plays significant physiological roles in vivo. GSH is distributed in animals and plants [6]. The normal concentration of GSH in animal cells ranges from 0.5 to 10 mM, and the normal concentration of GSH in physiological fluids ranges from 2 to 12 μM. However, abnormal levels of GSH are associated with many diseases such as heart problems, liver damage, cancer and other ailment [7]. Owing to its functions mentioned above, the selective and sensitive techniques for GSH detection are of great importance. So far, considerable efforts have been devoted to detect GSH. In general, these techniques include gas chromatographic method (GC), electrochemistry, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [8]. However, there are still some hindrances, such as time-consuming process and complicated operation. Therefore, novel strategies with high sensitivity, simple operability and on-line detection are highly desirable for GSH detection.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a new class of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles, have attracted considerable interests due to their prominent properties in terms of chemical inertness, easy surface modification, nontoxicity, photostability and biocompatibility [9]. Based on these significant advantages, CDs have made great progress in a variety of applications, such as chemical sensors, optoelectronic devices and biomedicine [10,11]. The fluorescence of CDs was observed to be turned-off in the presence of metal ions, and restored after the formation of complexes between the metal ion and the chelators [12]. Based on this mechanism, a lot of PL “off-on” sensors have been developed from CDs for the detection of chelators, most of them are important in biology. To prepare CDs with adequate quantum yields (QYs) of PL, surface passivation and chemical doping have been used to improve the properties of CDs [13,14]. The previous studies have showed that the functional group containing N and O doped in CDs may improve their ability of chelation with metal ions, leading to an efficient quenching and then can be recovered by chelators [15,16]. The solvent-free one-pot solid state method is fast, effective and environmentally friendly. Many works related to the synthesis of NCDs by this method has been reported before, He et al. and Rong et al. previously reported that nitrogen-doped carbon points synthesized by solid state method has a great effect of detection for Fe3+ [17,18].

In this paper, a novel nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) for ultrasensitive and visual quantitative detection of Hg2+and GSH was developed by reacting anhydrous citric acid as the carbon source and the nitrogen-rich 2-amino-1 H-benzimidazole as the surface passivation for nitrogen source without the use of any other acid, alkali, organic solvents or water by solid state method. The synthesized NCDs have been proved to be an effective fluorescent sensor, which can detect Hg2+ ions by fluorescence quenching and determine glutathione by fluorescence recovery through the “on-off-on” process. The synthetic route of NCDs and the sensing mechanism for Hg2+ and GSH detection were shown in Scheme 1. In addition, the prepared NCDs hydrogel sensor was successfully applied to analyze Hg2+ and GSH in real water samples and blood serums with naked eyes. This novel NCDs hydrogel sensor can be easily conducted without fluorophotometer-based calibration and provides an effective route to facilitate the evolution of visual fluorescent sensors from qualitative to quantitative detection.

Anhydrous citric acid was purchased from Guangfu Reagent Company (Tianjin, China). 2-aminobenzimidazole (98%) was purchased from Sinopharm chemical Regent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). The chlorides of (Li+, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Ba2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Zn2+and Fe2+), CuSO4, and AgNO3 were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Glutathione (GSH) and other amino acids include: glutamic acid (Glu), arginine (Arg), glycine (Gly), lysine (Lys), aspartic acid (Asn), glutamine (Gln), leucine (Leu), isoleucine (Ile), phenylalanine (Phe), proline (Pro), threonine (Thr), serine (Ser), tryptophan (Trp), tyrosine (Tyr), valine (Val) which were obtained from Macklin Biochemical Company (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were analytical grade and used without further purification. The water used in all experiments was double-distilled.

X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) spectra were measured on Rigaku SmartLab Diffractometer (Cu Kα radiation). The morphology and size of the CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM 2011, Japan) at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. The emission lifetimes and the time-resolved emission spectra were recorded using the time-correlated single-photon-counting method, and the excitation source was an nF900 ns flash lamp filled with hydrogen gas. The infrared spectra (FT-IR) were obtained by Nicolet IS50 FT-IR spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were recorded on a VG Multilab 2000 system with Al Ka radiation (Thermo VG Scientific) with 1 eV energy step. The absorbance spectra were taken from a Varian Cary 500 Spectrophotometer. The fluorescence spectra were recorded on a F-4600 spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan).

NCDs were prepared by solid state method without any reagent (acids, alkali, organic solvents or water). First, 0.1921 g anhydrous citric acid and 0.3995 g 2-aminobenzimidazole were well mixed and ground thoroughly with an agate mortar. The solid powder was then transferred to a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and reacted at 180 ℃ for 5 h. After the autoclave cooled to room temperature, the black-green solids were transferred to 100 mL ultra-pure water for ultrasonic dispersion for 10 min and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min to remove the unreacted materials. The supernatant of NCDs was taken and dialyzed overnight with molecular weight 500 cut-off dialysis membranes. Next, the NCDs solution in the dialysis bag was transferred to a glass container, frozen in the refrigerator for one day and freeze-dried in the lyophilizer for two days. The final product was yellow-green solid powder, yield, 88.64% (the mass ratio of product to the total reaction materials). Finally, the yellow-green powder was redissolved to 50 μg/mL with ultra-pure water and stored at 4 ℃ in refrigerator for further characterization and application.

The fluorescence quantum yield (Q) was determined using quinine sulfate as reference (Qs = 54%, 0.1 M H2SO4) according to the reported method [19]. The Q value was calculated according to the following Eq. (1):
(1)
Q ( % ) =
Q
s
×
S
Ss
×
n
2
n
s
2
× 100 %
Here, “S” and “Ss” are fluorescence peak area of samples and quinine sulfate, “n” and “ns” are the refractive indexes of the solvents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of real water samples and human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: The water of Jing Lake in Northeast Normal University is filtered three times by filter paper to remove large impurities. Then, the filtrate was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min and the supernatant was used as water sample. In addition, tap water as water sample was also directly used.

The obtained human blood sample was stored in a 10 mL centrifuge tube without adding anticoagulant and was left standing at 37 ℃ until natural coagulation. The coagulated human blood sample was balanced steadily and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, and the obtained supernatant was stored in refrigerator at 4 ℃ as the human serum sample. Finally, the prepared human serum sample solution was for subsequent testing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the novel NCDs hydrogel film sensor
ce:section.ce:para: 0.1 g the prepared NCDs (yellow-green solid product) was dispersed in 50 mL ultra-pure water in a beaker. Then, 1.0 g carrageenan and 0.4 g locust bean gum were successively added into the NCDs solution and stirred well immediately to prevent condensation. Finally, 0.1 g CaCl2 was added to the above mixture. The beaker containing the mixture is placed in a constant temperature water bath at 80 ℃ for 20 min. Then the prepared colloid is quickly and evenly spread on the surface of circular dishes, so that the glue liquid is well spread to form a thin layer and cooled until it is completely solidified into NCDs hydrogel films. These films were carefully removed along the edge of the surface dish. The films were irradiated under 365 nm ultraviolet light and the blue fluorescence colors of films were observed. The NCDs films prepared above were soaked in different concentrations of Hg2+ solution and then irradiated under 365 nm ultraviolet light to observe the changes in their fluorescence colors. Additionally, the hydrogel films for detection of GSH were prepared as above procedure with the addition of Hg2+ water solution (50 μL, 20 mmol/L) and the visual quantitative detection of GSH is also conducted in the similar way.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were carried out to study the size and morphology of NCDs. As shown in Fig. 1a, the obtained particles are irregularly spherical in shape and show good homogeneity in the particle size distribution without apparent aggregation. In addition, we also studied the crystalline properties of NCDs by high resolution (HRTEM) measurement. As shown in Fig. 1b, the crystal lattice spacing of NCDs is 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the planar lattice of (100) graphite [20] and indicates the prepared NCDs has great crystallinity.

The prepared NCDs solid powder was characterized by XRD to study its crystallinity. According to the XRD pattern of NCDs as shown in Fig. 2a, a broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 24° in the range of 13–40° can be observed. This broad peak is due to the small size of NCDs. It explains the amorphous nature of NCDs, which consists of highly disordered carbon nuclei and amorphous surface structure, thus confirming the successful synthesis of NCDs in this study.

The surface functional groups of NCDs were investigated with the FT-IR spectra. As seen the FT-IR spectrum in Fig. 2b, the absorption peak at 3420 cm−1 represents the stretching vibration of the O-H bond and -COOH. In addition, a sharply characteristic peak at 1690 cm−1 of the amide group (-CONH) formed by the condensation of the amino group (-NH2) on the 2-aminobenzimidazole with the partial carboxyl group on the anhydrous citric acid was observed. These bands indicate that the main passivation groups on the surface of NCDs are hydroxyl, carboxyl and amide groups. Meanwhile, these functional groups improve the hydrophilicity of NCDs in pure water. A strong band at the wavenumber of 1580 cm−1 is ascribed to the CO stretching frequency. The peaks at 1270 cm−1 can be ascribed to the CN stretching frequencies.

The compositions of NCDs were further confirmed by full XPS measurement. As shown in Fig. 2c, the three peaks at 287 eV, 399 eV and 532 eV were attributed to O1 s, N1 s and C1 s, respectively. The contents of O, C and doped N were calculated with the relative sensitivity factor method to be 22.86%, 55.67% and 12.04%, respectively. The high resolution XPS spectra of C1s, O1s, N1s of NCDs were shown in Fig.S1. The peaks at 285.62 eV, 286.69 eV, 287.93 eV, 284.56 eV and 283.96 eV correspond to C-N, CN, COOH, CO and C-C/CC bonds, respectively. The peaks of O1s at 530.58 eV, 531.54 eV, 532.38 eV are ascribed to -OH, CO and O-CO, respectively. The N1s peaks are severally at 399.03 eV (C-N), 400.48 eV (CN) and 399.73 eV (N-H). These results confirms that the main components of NCDs are N, C and O elements and the nitrogen-doped carbon dots using 2-aminobenzimidazole as nitrogen-containing precursor has been successfully synthesized.

The NCDs aqueous solution has a strong absorption band at 235–325 nm with the peak at 275 nm in its UV–vis spectrum (Fig.S2), which could be assigned to the π–π * transition of graphitic sp2 domains [8]. The prepared NCDs also exhibited the characteristic fluorescence property, its fluorescence spectra red-shifted with the increase of excitation wavelengths in the range of 335–425 nm. As shown in Fig. 2d, the fluorescence spectra of NCDs gradually red-shifted with the increase of excitation wavelengths, and the fluorescence intensity was the maximum under the excitation of 375 nm. Therefore, 375 nm was selected as the excitation wavelength in the later fluorescence measurements of NCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The “on-off” fluorescence sensing of NCDs for Hg2+ in water
ce:section.ce:para: The groups of -OH, -COOH and -NH2 on the surface of NCDs have good chelating ability to metal ions, and the coordination of metal ion will affect the fluorescence of NCDs. Therefore, the fluorescence response of NCDs in different solvents (N, N-dimethylformamide DMF, ethanol EtOH, methyl cyanide MeCN and water) towards the different metal ions including Li+, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Ba2+, Hg2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Cu2+ and Ag+ was first investigated. 50 μL of different metal ion solutions (20 mmol/L) were respectively added to 5 mL NCDs dispersion (50 μg/mL) in the abovementioned solvents, their fluorescence spectra were measured under excitation of 375 nm after mixed for 30 s. As described in the supporting information S1 and Fig.S3-S6. Only in water, NCDs presented selective fluorescence quenching response towards Hg2+ over the other tested metal ions. As shown in Fig. 3a and Fig.S6, only Hg2+ caused obvious fluorescence quenching of NCDs, the quantum yield of NCDs declined from 11.17% to 1.71%. On the contrary, the other tested metal ions have little effect on the fluorescence spectra of NCDs. Therefore, water was selected as solvent of NCDs to study the fluorescence sensing performance for Hg2+. The fluorescence photographs of these solutions under the UV lamp further verified the selective fluorescence quenching of NCDs upon the addition of Hg2+. As shown in Fig.S7, the bright blue fluorescence color of only the Hg2+-containing solution disappeared. In addition, NCDs have fast fluorescence response times towards Hg2+ and can reached to equilibrium state within 30 s (Fig.S8), indicating that NCDs can detect Hg2+ on-site. To further verify the high fluorescence response selectivity of NCDs towards Hg2+, the common anions including Br-, CH3COO-, NO2 -, NO3 -, ClO3 -, ClO-, CO3 2-, HCO3 -, Cl-, F-, S2-, SO3 2-, SO4 2-, H2PO4 -, HSO3 - and I- were also tested and they almost have no influence on the fluorescence intensity of NCDs (Fig.S9).

The highly selective fluorescence response of NCDs towards Hg2+ can be attributed to the stronger complexation of Hg2+ with the functional groups such as -OH, -NH2, -COOH and -CONH on the surface of NCDs than the other competitive metal ions. After complex formation, the electron transfer from NCDs to the unoccupied orbitals of Hg2+ induced the significant fluorescence quenching of NCDs [10,21]. The competitive experiment further confirmed the selective fluorescence response. When Hg2+ and other several metal ions with the same concentration (50 μL, 20 mmol/L) were together added to NCDs water solution（5 mL, 50 μg/mL）, the fluorescence intensity at 425 nm of NCDs was still quenched obviously in the presence of the most of metal ions tested (Fig. 3b). Only Al3+, Cr3+ and Ni2+ exhibited a little influence on the fluorescence quenching effect of Hg2+. This result indicated that the prepared NCDs has excellent anti-interference ability and the coexistence of the other metal ions don’t affect the fluorescence sensing of NCDs for Hg2+. These results indicate that the prepared NCDs have high fluorescence response selectivity towards Hg2+ and the other coexisting ions don’t interfere with the identification of NCDs for Hg2+ ions.

The sensing sensitivity of NCDs to Hg2+ was elevated through the fluorescence titration experiment. As shown in Fig. 3c and d, the fluorescence spectra of NCDs in water were gradually quenched with the incremental increase of the concentration of Hg2+ accompanied by a little bule-shift. The fluorescence intensity at 425 nm of NCDs gradually weakened with the increase of Hg2+ concentration in the range of 0–60 μM, and had a good linear relationship with the concentration of Hg2+ in the concentration range of 12–28 μM with a high correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9933. The low detection limit can be calculated to be 3.50 μM according to the equation of LOD = 3.3 × δ/k, this value is in the upper reaches of those reported in the literature (Table S1). Here, δ is the standard deviation of blank samples, k is the slope of the titration curve). The results proved that the prepared NCDs have the excellent selectivity and sensitivity in the “turn-off” fluorescence identification and detection of Hg2+ and can be used as fluorescence sensor of Hg2+ in water solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The “turn-on” fluorescence sensing of NCDs@Hg2+ for GSH
ce:section.ce:para: Hg2+ ions tend to form strong coordination bond with S atoms and some of the common amino acid contains S atoms. If the Hg2+ was snatched from the NCDs@Hg2+ complex, it is reasonable to predict that the quenched fluorescence of NCDs by Hg2+ could be restored. Therefore, the NCDs@ Hg2+ system in water was prepared by mixing the NCDs solution (5 mL, 50 μg/mL) and Hg2+ (50 μL, 20 mmol/L). The fluorescence recovery rate of NCDs is fast and the fluorescence intensity can reach the maximum within 1 min as exhibited in Fig.S10. Different kinds of amino acid solutions (50 μL, 20 mmol/L) including glutathione and other amino acids include: glutamic acid, arginine, glycine, lysine, aspartic acid, glutamine, leucine, isoleucine, phenylalanine, proline, threonine, serine, tryptophan, tyrosine and valine were severally added into the 5 mL NCDs@Hg2+ in water solution, and their fluorescence spectra were measured under the excitation of 375 nm after mixed for 2 min. As shown in Fig. 4a, only GSH caused the obvious fluorescence enhancement of NCDs@Hg2+ (Fig.S11) and the quantum yield of the solution was restored to 8.53%. On the contrary, the other amino acids did not give obvious influence on the fluorescence, which indicated that NCDs@Hg2+ has high fluorescence response selectivity towards GSH over the other tested amino acids. In other word, only GSH can snatch Hg2+ from NCDs@Hg2+ complexes and release the free NCDs nanoparticles. In addition, the changes of the fluorescence photographs of these solutions illustrated the changes of the fluorescence spectra, only GSH-containing solution of NCDs@Hg2+ presented the bright blue fluorescence. The comparison of the fluorescence photographs of these solutions under the UV lamp further confirmed the selective fluorescence recovery of NCDs@Hg2+, only the addition of GSH caused the appearance of the bright blue fluorescence color (inset in Fig. 4a). Next, the interference experiments of the other amino acids were carried out, 50 μL of GSH and other several amino acids (20 mmol/L) were added to 5 mL NCDs@Hg2+ system simultaneously and their fluorescence spectra were measured. The fluorescence intensities of the solutions at 425 nm still significantly increased even if the existence of the other competitive amino acids (Fig. 4b), which further confirmed the other amino acids don’t interference with the fluorescence enhancement effect of GSH on the fluorescence of NCDs@Hg2+ system.

The sensing sensitivity of NCDs@Hg2+ for GSH was then evaluated by the fluorescence titration experiment. As shown in Fig. 4c, the fluorescence spectra gradually increased with the incremental additions of GSH accompanied by the slight red-shift. The fluorescence intensity at 425 nm showed a good linear relationship against the concentration of GSH in the range of 40–120 μM with a high correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9957 (Fig. 4d). Based on this calibration curve, the low detection limit (LOD) of GSH has been calculated to be 3.43 μM according to the formula of 3δ/k, which ranks at upstream compared with those reported in the literature (Table S2). Above experiments confirm that NCDs@Hg2+ system has very high fluorescence selectivity and good sensitivity and can be used as “turn-on” fluorescence sensor of GSH.

The fluorescence of the carbon dots origin from the surface state, and the doping different heteroatoms (e.g., N, S, B, P, etc.) in the carbon dots can modulate the their surface energy levels and further improves the fluorescence property [22]. The doping of nitrogen-rich molecule can reduce the ratio of defective state luminescence and non-radiative transition at carbon dots, as well as raises band gap transitions in the conjugated π interval of eigenstates.

The “on-off-on” fluorescence changes are ascribed to the successive formation and dissociation of NCDs@Hg2+ complex. To confirm this speculation, the UV–vis absorbance spectra of NCDs, NCDs+Hg2+, NCDs@Hg2++GSH were measured and compared (Fig.S2). The NCDs solution has a strong absorption peak at 275 nm and did not have obvious change after addition of Hg2+, but the addition of Hg2+ caused the obvious absorption after 285 nm indicating the interaction of NCDs with Hg2+. After formation of NCDs@Hg2+ complex, the non-radiative electron-transfer from NCDs to Hg2+ could be happening and quenched the fluorescence of NCDs [12]. After further addition of GSH, the absorption band caused by the addition of Hg2+ disappeared and the absorbance spectrum roughly returned to the original level, which indicated the detaching of Hg2+ from NCDs@Hg2+ complex. After Hg2+ of NCDs@Hg2+ was captured by GSH, the fluorescence of NCDs was restored. The spectral changes verified the formation and dissociation process of NCDs@Hg2+ complex after successive additions of Hg2+ and GSH. The fluorescence sensing mechanism can be expressed as shown in Scheme 1.

Stern-Volmer diagrams, I
0
/I = 1 + k
sv [Q], of fluorescence quenching of NCDs at different concentrations of Hg2+ are drawn, as shown in Fig. 5a and Fig.S12. Here, Ksv is the association equilibrium constant, I0 and I are the fluorescence intensity of NCDs with and without Hg2+, respectively. The Stern-Volmer curve show nonlinear curves in the range of 0–60 μM and good linear relationships in the range of 12–28 μM, which indicates that the fluorescence quenching mechanism of NCDs by Hg2+ involves not only static quenching and but also dynamic quenching [23]. Static quenching can be attributed to the interaction between NCDs with Hg2+ and the formed complexes are non-fluorescent at ground state. The dynamic quenching is caused between NCDs with Hg2+ at excited states [24,25], the excited states of NCDs lose their excitation energy by colliding with each other through the energy transfer or charge transfer and return the ground state.

The time-correlated single-photon-counting measurements were conducted to study the fluorescence decay behavior of NCDs and can better interpret the fluorescence change mechanism [26]. The fluorescence attenuation curves of NCDs, NCDs+Hg2+ and NCDs@Hg2++GSH were fitted by the double exponential model is shown in Fig. 5b. The average fluorescence lifetime “τ” of NCDs, NCDs+Hg2+ and NCDs@Hg2++GSH are calculated to be 6.98 ns, 6.36 ns and 5.59 ns, respectively. The obvious decrease of fluorescence lifetime of NCDs after the addition of Hg2+ indicated that the fluorescence quenching is mainly caused by the dynamic quenching. The results in this case further confirms that fluorescence quenching mainly involves dynamic quenching process but also small amount of static quenching process [27].

To further explore the feasibility of NCDs in detecting Hg2+ and GSH in real water samples, we conducted a series of spiked recovery experiments on tap water and the Jing Lake water (Northeast Normal University, Changchun, China). Two pre-filtered water samples were added the standard solution of Hg2+ and GSH with different concentrations respectively. The measurement results of the spiked Hg2+ were presented in Table 1. As we can see, the recovery values ranged from 99.93% to 106.35% with the relative standard deviations (RSD) from 0.58% to 0.78% in the Jing Lake water, and the recovery values ranged from 101.71% to 113.66% with the RSD (n = 3) values from 0.44% to 1.70% in the tap water. Moreover, as shown in Table 2, the recovery range of GSH is 98.04–106.09% with the RSD values from 0.67% to 0.69% in the Jing Lake water, and the recovery values of GSH ranged from 98.86% to 101.20% with the RSD values from 0.52% to 1.34% in the tap water. The above results show that the spiked values are consistent with the measured data. It indicates that NCDs has great potential for the quantitative detection of Hg2+ and GSH in actual water samples.

The monitoring of mercuric (II) and glutathione in biological samples is necessary due to their significant influence on the health of the organism [28,29]. So the prepared NCDs were also used to measure Hg2+ and GSH in human serum samples to further evaluate their practical application. The spiked recovery experiments of Hg2+ and GSH were operated in human serum samples severally. The measured concentrations of mercuric (II) and glutathione in un-spiked and spiked samples are displayed in Tables 3 and 4, The recovery rates of Hg2+ were between 99.47% and 107.90% with the RSD values from 0.66% to 0.89%. And the recovery rates of Hg2+ were between 99.93% and 106.35% with the RSD values from 0.58% to 0.78%. The high recovery values proved the prepared NCDs can be used to identify and detect Hg2+ and glutathione in biological samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual quantitative detection of Hg2+and GSH
ce:section.ce:para: In order to realize the detection of Hg2+ and GSH by the prepared NCDs in a simple and feasible way, two kinds of new hydrogel sensor films composed of carrageenan and locust bean gum were developed as the preparation steps descripted earlier in the experiment section. One hydrogel film only contained NCDs (named FNCD) and another contained both NCDs and Hg2+ (named FNCD@Hg).

After immersed in Hg2+ solution in water with different concentrations, the blue fluorescence colors of FNCD hydrogel films under UV lamp gradually weakened with the increase of Hg2+ concentration in micromolar level ( Fig. 6a). The differences among these fluorescence colors were easy to identify with the naked eyes [30–32], thus the micromolar level semi-quantitative detection for Hg2+ in the test film mode can be realized.

As exhibited in Fig. 6b, the blue fluorescence color of FNCD@Hg hydrogel films obviously increased with the increase of the concentration of GSH in micromolar level under the 365 nm UV lamp. This result indicated that FNCD@Hg hydrogel films also can be used to semi-quantitative detection of GSH with the naked eyes in the simple way.

Based on the above results, it can be concluded that the prepared NCDs can be used as fluorescence sensor to semi-quantitative detection of Hg2+ and GSH in the simple hydrogel film mode with the naked eyes and has great application prospect in the practical water and biological samples.

To sum up, a novel nitrogen-doped carbon dots have been successfully synthesized using solid state method. TEM images indicate that NCDs particles exhibits irregular spherical shape, the results of XRD, XPS and IR prove that nitrogen is successfully doped in the carbon dots (NCDs). The prepared NCDs have favorable fluorescence quantum yields and can successively identify and detect Hg2+and GSH in fluorescence “turn-off” and “turn-on” modes accompanied by the significant fluorescence color changes. NCDs have high selectivity over the many other coexistence interfering substance and high sensitivity with the low detection limits of 3.50 μM for Hg2+ and 3.43 μM for GSH. In addition, the prepared NCDs have satisfactory recoveries in the fluorescence detection of Hg2+ and GSH in the water samples and human serum samples indicating its high application potentials. Furthermore, NCDs can identify and semi-quantitative detect Hg2+and GSH in micromolar level with the naked eyes in the simple mode of hydrogel films. The results of this work further proved that the N-doping carbon dots are efficient for the identification and detection of metal ion and bioactive substance in practical water and biological samples.

Guang Wang designed the study and rephrased the manuscript, Yuxin Han did the experiments, collected and analyzed test data, wrote the manuscript, Yuying Bian collected and analyzed a part of experimental data.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (no. 22075040). The authors also acknowledge the supports from Jilin Provincial Department of Education.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2023.110750.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.
